writing_simple_readable_queries.label: Writing Simple Readable Queries
writing_simple_readable_queries.summary: Learn how to write clear beginner SELECT queries that are easy to read and return the columns you want from the products table.

sketch1.title: Choose columns in a clear order

sc_select_clause_role.title: What SELECT does
sc_select_clause_role.prompt: In a query like `SELECT name, price FROM products;`, what does the `SELECT` part do?

help.concept: `SELECT` chooses which columns appear in the results table.
help.hint_1: In this query, `name` and `price` are written right after `SELECT`.
help.hint_2: Ask yourself which part controls the visible fields in the output, not the source table.

options.a: It chooses which columns to show
options.b: It chooses which table to read

sc_from_clause_role.title: What FROM does
sc_from_clause_role.prompt: In `SELECT name FROM products;`, what does `FROM products` tell SQL?
sc_from_clause_role.hint: It points SQL to the data source.

help.concept: `FROM` names the table that SQL should read.
help.hint_1: The word after `FROM` is the table name in this lesson.
help.hint_2: Think about where SQL gets the `name` values before showing them in the results table.

mc_readable_query_habits.title: Readable query habits
mc_readable_query_habits.prompt: Which choices help make a beginner SQL query more readable? Choose all that apply.
mc_readable_query_habits.hint: Think about layout and clarity, not advanced features.

help.concept: Readable SQL uses clear clause order and formatting so the query is easier to scan.
help.hint_1: Consider whether putting major clauses on separate lines helps someone read the query.
help.hint_2: Look for choices about clear formatting and explicit column lists rather than unrelated SQL behavior.

options.c: List needed columns instead of always using `*`
options.d: Replace the table name with any word you like

mc_select_star_truths.title: Understanding SELECT *
mc_select_star_truths.prompt: Which statements about `SELECT * FROM products;` are correct? Choose all that apply.
mc_select_star_truths.hint: Think about what `*` means in a SELECT list.

help.concept: In SQL, `*` means all columns from the table named in `FROM`.
help.hint_1: This query returns every column in `products`, not just one or two.
help.hint_2: Check which choices describe all columns and which ones incorrectly describe filtering or sorting.

options.a: It returns all columns from the `products` table
options.b: It can be useful for quickly inspecting the whole table
options.d: It means the same thing as selecting only `name` and `price`

dr_query_clause_order_basic.title: Build the query in order
dr_query_clause_order_basic.prompt: Arrange these tokens to form a readable query that shows `name` and `price` from `products`.
dr_query_clause_order_basic.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

dr_query_clause_order_star.title: Order a full-table query
dr_query_clause_order_star.prompt: Arrange these tokens to make a query that returns every column from `products`.
dr_query_clause_order_star.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

fb_table_name_products.title: Fill in the table name
fb_table_name_products.prompt: Complete the query so it reads from the products table.
fb_table_name_products.hint: Use the table name shown in the schema browser.

help.concept: The `FROM` clause must name the table that contains the columns you want to read.
help.hint_1: This lesson uses one table with columns like `id`, `name`, and `price`.
help.hint_2: Choose the table name that matches the dataset schema, not a column name.

fb_star_for_all_columns.title: Fill in all columns
fb_star_for_all_columns.prompt: Complete the query so it returns every column from `products`.
fb_star_for_all_columns.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

ci_select_name_price.title: Show product names and prices
ci_select_name_price.prompt: Write a readable SQL query that returns the `name` and `price` columns from the `products` table. Put `SELECT` and `FROM` on separate lines. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check the results table.
ci_select_name_price.hint: Use the query pattern from the lesson.

help.concept: This SQL exercise checks whether your query returns the requested result.
help.hint_1: Check the table name and selected columns in the SQL editor.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the results table.

ci_select_all_columns.title: Inspect the whole table
ci_select_all_columns.prompt: Write a query that returns every column from the `products` table. Use a readable two-line format in the SQL editor, then click Run query and check the results table.
ci_select_all_columns.hint: Use the query pattern from the lesson.

help.concept: This SQL exercise checks whether your query returns the requested result.
help.hint_1: Check the table name and selected columns in the SQL editor.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the results table.

ci_select_name_category_stock.title: Choose three useful columns
ci_select_name_category_stock.prompt: Write a readable query that returns `name`, `category`, and `stock` from the `products` table. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check that the results table shows those columns in that order.
ci_select_name_category_stock.hint: Use the query pattern from the lesson.

help.concept: This SQL exercise checks whether your query returns the requested result.
help.hint_1: Check the table name and selected columns in the SQL editor.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the results table.

try-writing_simple_readable_queries-sket.title: Query name and price from products
try-writing_simple_readable_queries-sket.prompt: Build a product-catalog query that returns `name`, `price` from the `products` table. Run it and compare the result with the expected table.
try-writing_simple_readable_queries-sket.hint: Use the query pattern from the lesson, then adjust the selected columns, table, or filter for this scenario.

help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the result table with the expected result.

try-writing_simple_readable_queries-sket.title: Query all products rows
try-writing_simple_readable_queries-sket.prompt: Build a product-catalog query that returns all columns from the `products` table. Run it and compare the result with the expected table.
try-writing_simple_readable_queries-sket.hint: Use the query pattern from the lesson, then adjust the selected columns, table, or filter for this scenario.

help.concept: This SQL exercise checks whether your query returns the requested result.
help.hint_1: Check the selected columns and table name in the SQL editor.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the result table with the expected result.

try-writing_simple_readable_queries-sket.title: Write a Readable Inventory Query
try-writing_simple_readable_queries-sket.prompt: Return `name`, `category`, and `stock` from `products`, one logical clause per line.
try-writing_simple_readable_queries-sket.hint: Add stock to the SELECT list before FROM.

help.concept: Return `name`, `category`, and `stock` from `products`, one logical clause per line.
help.hint_1: Add stock to the SELECT list before FROM.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the returned columns and rows with the expected result.

sketch_select_from_basics.title: Start with SELECT and FROM
sketch_select_from_basics.bodyMarkdown:
A simple readable SQL query usually answers two questions:

1. **What columns do I want to see?** → `SELECT`
2. **Which table should SQL read from?** → `FROM`

Here is a beginner-friendly example using the `products` table:

```sql
SELECT name, price
FROM products;
```

Line by line:

- `SELECT name, price` asks for the `name` and `price` columns.
- `FROM products` tells SQL which table to read.
- `;` ends the query.

If you run this in the SQL editor, the results table will show product names and prices only, not every column in the table.

A readable query puts each main clause on its own line. That makes it easier to scan and edit later.

sketch_column_order_and_star.title: Choose columns in a clear order
sketch_column_order_and_star.bodyMarkdown:
The order of columns in `SELECT` becomes the order of columns in the results table.

Example:

```sql
SELECT id, name, category
FROM products;
```

This returns the columns in this order:

1. `id`
2. `name`
3. `category`

You can also ask for every column with `*`:

```sql
SELECT *
FROM products;
```

That is useful when you want a quick look at the whole table. In the `products` table, this shows `id`, `name`, `category`, `price`, `stock`, and `created_at`.

For example, one row includes:

- `1`
- `Sketchbook`
- `Art`
- `12.99`
- `25`
- `2026-01-10`

But when you only need a few columns, listing them is usually more readable than using `*`.

sketch_readable_formatting.title: Make queries easy to read
sketch_readable_formatting.bodyMarkdown:
Readable SQL is not just correct SQL. It should also be easy for you and other people to understand.

Compare these two versions:

```sql
SELECT name, category, price FROM products;
```

```sql
SELECT name, category, price
FROM products;
```

Both work, but the second version is easier to read because each main part has its own line.

Here is another worked example:

```sql
SELECT name, stock, created_at
FROM products;
```

Step by step:

- `name` shows the product name.
- `stock` shows how many are available.
- `created_at` shows the stored date text.
- `FROM products` reads from the only table in this lesson.

A good beginner habit is:

- put `SELECT` first
- separate column names with commas
- put `FROM` on the next line
- end with a semicolon
